slides.title: The Toolbox: Types of Security Controls
slides.subtitle: Cyber Foundations: every security control protects confidentiality, integrity, or availability — and can be classified by type (physical, technical, managerial) and by function (preventive, detective, corrective)
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Lesson 2.1, Day 6 of 8. Yesterday, 2.1.E, mitigation meant 'implement security controls to reduce a risk.' Today, 2.1.F, we open the toolbox and learn what those controls actually are and how the College Board classifies them. There are two classification systems, and the exam expects you to use both. First, by type: physical, technical, and managerial — where the control lives. Second, by function: preventive, detective, and corrective — what the control does about an attack. And underneath all of it, every control serves at least one of three principles: confidentiality, integrity, or availability. By the end of class you'll be able to take any control — a lock, a firewall, a password policy — and classify it both ways.

slides.prompt: Think about how a bank protects its money. There's the locked vault and the security guard out front. There's the alarm system and the cameras that record everyone who walks in. And there's the rule that no single employee can open the vault alone.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: these are all 'security controls,' but they aren't the same kind of thing. Sort the vault, guard, alarm, cameras, and the two-person rule into groups that feel alike to you, and say what makes each group different.
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own. Picture how a bank guards its money: a locked vault and a guard out front, an alarm and cameras recording everyone, and a rule that no one employee can open the vault alone. In three to five sentences, sort those into groups that feel alike and say what makes each group different. You're about to discover you naturally grouped them two ways — by where they live and by what they do — which is exactly how the College Board classifies controls. This is EK 2.1.F.2 and F.3.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 6 Guided Notes have three sections that match today’s lesson. All are CB-required.

sections.title: What Controls Protect: C-I-A
sections.description: Every security control addresses at least one of confidentiality, integrity, or availability (EK 2.1.F.1).

sections.title: Classified by Type
sections.description: Controls live somewhere: physical, technical, or managerial controls each protect a different space (EK 2.1.F.2).

sections.title: Classified by Function
sections.description: Controls do something: preventive, detective, and corrective controls each act at a different point in an attack (EK 2.1.F.3).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.1 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Three sections today, all CB-required. First, what every control protects — confidentiality, integrity, or availability, the C-I-A principles. Second, controls classified by type: physical, technical, managerial — where the control lives. Third, controls classified by function: preventive, detective, corrective — what the control does about an attack. The big idea: the same control gets classified BOTH ways at once. Keep the packet open; I collect Guided Notes at the end.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Name the three CIA principles every control serves and match a missing principle to its harm — theft, manipulation, or downtime (LO 2.1.F)
Classify a control by type — physical, technical, or managerial — by where it lives (LO 2.1.F)
Classify the same control by function — preventative, detective, or corrective — labeling it on both independent axes at once (LO 2.1.F)

slides.script: Three things to leave with today, all under learning objective 2.1.F, identify types of security controls. First, name the three CIA principles every control serves — confidentiality, integrity, availability — and match a missing principle to its harm: no confidentiality means theft, no integrity means manipulation, no availability means downtime. Second, classify a control by type, by where it lives: physical in the physical space, technical in the digital space, managerial in the rules and policies. Third, classify that same control by function, by what it does: preventative stops the attack, detective spots it, corrective fixes the damage — and label it on both axes at once, because the exam asks for both. By the end of today you can place any control in both systems.

slides.label: Types of Security Controls
slides.script: Section six of the lesson. Learning objective 2.1.F. First what controls protect, then the two ways to classify them. As we go, keep asking two questions of every control: where does it live, and what does it do? Fourteen minutes here.

slides.heading: What Every Control Protects: The C-I-A Principles
slides.subheading: EK 2.1.F.1 — a security control addresses at least one of confidentiality, integrity, or availability. Lacking one creates a specific vulnerability.

left.title: The Three Principles (EK 2.1.F.1)
left.definition: What security controls exist to protect
examples: Confidentiality: only authorized individuals, systems, or processes can access data
Integrity: data are accurate and trustworthy
Availability: data and services are accessible to authorized individuals when needed
A control must address at least one of these three

right.title: What Goes Wrong Without Each
right.definition: The vulnerability when a principle is missing
examples: No confidentiality → vulnerable to data theft or destruction
No integrity → vulnerable to data manipulation
No availability → may experience unexpected downtime
Each principle maps to a distinct kind of harm

slides.footer: AP TIP: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the C-I-A triad. Every security control addresses at least one (EK 2.1.F.1). Match the missing principle to its harm: confidentiality→theft, integrity→manipulation, availability→downtime.
slides.script: Before we classify controls, know what they're for. EK 2.1.F.1 says every security control addresses at least one of three principles. Left column: confidentiality ensures only authorized individuals, systems, or processes can access data; integrity ensures data are accurate and trustworthy; availability ensures data and services are accessible to authorized people when needed. Right column is the exam's favorite move — what happens when each is missing. No confidentiality and you're vulnerable to data theft or destruction. No integrity and you're vulnerable to data manipulation. No availability and you get unexpected downtime. Memorize the triad and memorize each failure mode — the College Board pairs them constantly. Write both columns with EK 2.1.F.1.

slides.heading: Classified by TYPE: Where a Control Lives
slides.subheading: EK 2.1.F.2 — security controls can be classified by type. Physical, technical, and managerial each protect a different space.

items.title: Physical Controls
items.summary: Physical controls provide security in the physical space and include locks, fences, cameras, bollards, and security guards. They protect the building, the hardware, and physical access to systems.

items.title: Technical Controls
items.summary: Technical controls provide security in the digital space and include firewalls, anti-malware software, and encryption. They protect data and systems through technology rather than physical barriers.

items.title: Managerial Controls
items.summary: Managerial controls provide rules, guidelines, policies, and procedures that specify what security should be in place, and include password policies, regular access reviews, and incident response plans (IRPs).

slides.script: First classification, by type — where the control lives. All three are EK 2.1.F.2. Physical controls provide security in the physical space: locks, fences, cameras, bollards, security guards — anything protecting the building and the hardware. Technical controls provide security in the digital space: firewalls, anti-malware software, encryption — technology defending data and systems. Managerial controls are the rules: policies, guidelines, and procedures that specify what security should be in place — password policies, regular access reviews, incident response plans. A quick note on the objective wording: you'll sometimes see 'operational' listed as a fourth type, but the College Board's EK 2.1.F.2 names three — physical, technical, managerial — so those three are what you cite. Write all three with their examples.

slides.heading: Classified by FUNCTION: What a Control Does
slides.subheading: EK 2.1.F.3 — security controls can be classified by function. Preventive, detective, and corrective each act at a different moment in an attack.

items.title: Preventative Controls
items.summary: Preventative controls address potential vulnerabilities with the goal of stopping an adversary from attacking, and include locks and encryption. They act BEFORE an attack to keep it from succeeding.

items.title: Detective Controls
items.summary: Detective controls help identify attacks when they occur and include intrusion detection systems (IDSs), cameras, and security incident and event management (SIEM) systems. They act DURING an attack to spot it.

items.title: Corrective Controls
items.summary: Corrective controls fix problems and help restore systems to an operational state, and include vulnerability patching, repairing a broken card reader, and intrusion prevention systems (IPSs). They act AFTER to recover.

slides.script: Second classification, by function — what the control does, all EK 2.1.F.3. Think of an attack's timeline. Before: preventative controls address potential vulnerabilities to stop an adversary from attacking — locks and encryption. During: detective controls help identify attacks when they occur — intrusion detection systems, cameras, SIEM systems. After: corrective controls fix problems and restore systems to an operational state — vulnerability patching, repairing a broken card reader, intrusion prevention systems. Before, during, after — that's the cleanest way to keep them straight. And notice a control can be both: a camera is a physical control by type and a detective control by function. Write all three functions with their examples and EK 2.1.F.3.

slides.heading: The ‘A Control Has Just One Label’ Myth
slides.misconception: Each security control fits into exactly one category — a firewall is just 'technical,' a camera is just 'physical,' and that's the whole answer.
slides.whyItHappens: The two classification systems get taught as one long list, so students assume the categories are mutually exclusive slots and that naming one is naming all there is to say about a control.
slides.correction: EK 2.1.F.2 classifies by TYPE (where it lives: physical, technical, managerial) and EK 2.1.F.3 classifies by FUNCTION (what it does: preventative, detective, corrective). These are two independent axes, so every control gets a label on EACH. A camera is physical by type AND detective by function. Encryption is technical by type AND preventative by function. A lock is physical by type AND preventative by function.
slides.example: Take a SIEM system: by type it's technical (it lives in the digital space, EK 2.1.F.2), and by function it's detective (it identifies attacks as they occur, EK 2.1.F.3). Give only one label and you've answered half the question. The exam often asks you to classify a single control both ways in one prompt, so always check: where does it live, AND what does it do?
slides.script: Here's the misconception that costs points. Because the categories are taught as one list, students treat them as exclusive slots and give a control one label. But there are two independent classification systems. EK 2.1.F.2 is by type — where the control lives: physical, technical, managerial. EK 2.1.F.3 is by function — what it does: preventative, detective, corrective. Every control gets a label on each axis. A camera is physical by type and detective by function. Encryption is technical and preventative. A lock is physical and preventative. Take a SIEM: technical by type, detective by function — answer only one and you've answered half. The exam routinely asks you to classify one control both ways in a single prompt, so train the habit: where does it live, AND what does it do? Both, every time.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 2.1.F)

terms.definition: The three principles a security control must address at least one of — only authorized access (confidentiality), accurate and trustworthy data (integrity), and accessible data and services (availability) (EK 2.1.F.1).
A control that provides security in the physical space, such as locks, fences, cameras, bollards, and security guards (EK 2.1.F.2).
A control that provides security in the digital space, such as firewalls, anti-malware software, and encryption (EK 2.1.F.2).
A control that provides rules, guidelines, policies, and procedures, such as password policies, regular access reviews, and incident response plans (EK 2.1.F.2).
A control that addresses potential vulnerabilities to stop an adversary from attacking, such as locks and encryption (EK 2.1.F.3).
A control that helps identify attacks when they occur, such as intrusion detection systems, cameras, and SIEM systems (EK 2.1.F.3).
A control that fixes problems and restores systems to an operational state, such as vulnerability patching, repairing a card reader, and intrusion prevention systems (EK 2.1.F.3).

slides.script: Seven terms, the full classification vocabulary. Start with the C-I-A triad, the principles every control serves, EK 2.1.F.1. Then the three types, EK 2.1.F.2: physical lives in physical space, technical in digital space, managerial sets the rules. Then the three functions, EK 2.1.F.3: preventative stops the attack, detective spots it, corrective fixes the damage. For each function memorize its example controls — locks and encryption prevent, IDS and cameras and SIEM detect, patching and IPS correct. Get all seven into your notes with EK numbers, and remember every control takes one type label and one function label.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: All questions map to LO 2.1.F. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A company installs encryption on its laptops. Classify this control BOTH ways — by type and by function — citing the EK for each, and name which C-I-A principle it most directly protects.

An office has a security guard at the door, an intrusion detection system on the network, and a patching process that fixes flaws after they're found. Classify each of the three controls by FUNCTION (EK 2.1.F.3), and explain how together they cover before, during, and after an attack.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: laptop encryption — classify it both ways. By type it's technical, EK 2.1.F.2; by function it's preventative, EK 2.1.F.3; and the principle it most protects is confidentiality from F.1. Both labels and the principle. Question two: a guard, an IDS, and a patching process — classify each by function. The guard prevents, the IDS detects, the patching corrects — and explain how that covers before, during, and after an attack. Be precise with EK numbers.

slides.eyebrow: Your Turn — It's All on the Site
slides.heading: Your Turn

items.label: Lesson 2.1 page
items.tail: Review the lesson and run the self-check.

items.label: Exercise 2
items.tail: Classify controls by function: prevent / detect / correct.

slides.note: The lesson video covers this exact material, slide for slide.
slides.script: Everything is on the site. Re-run the Lesson 2.1 self-check, then Exercise 1, where you classify controls by type, and Exercise 2, where you classify them by function — prevent, detect, correct. The drill that earns points is classifying the same control both ways, so do plenty of them. The lesson video walks the material slide for slide if you want it again. Write the lesson page URL down now.

slides.heading: End of Day 6
todayPoints: Named the three principles every control serves: confidentiality, integrity, availability (EK 2.1.F.1)
Classified controls by TYPE — physical, technical, managerial — by where they live (EK 2.1.F.2)
Classified controls by FUNCTION — preventative, detective, corrective — by what they do (EK 2.1.F.3)
Practiced labeling a single control BOTH ways, since the two systems are independent axes
nextPoints: Learn why no single control is enough — and why layers matter (LO 2.1.G)
See how a defense-in-depth strategy gives resilience when one control is bypassed
Connect the control types and functions into layered protection
slides.teaser: You now have a toolbox full of controls — but a bank with only a vault and no alarm still gets robbed. Tomorrow you'll learn why defenders stack controls in layers, and what happens when an adversary slips past the first one.
slides.script: Day 6 done. You can name the C-I-A principles, classify any control by type — physical, technical, managerial — and by function — preventative, detective, corrective — and you can label the same control on both axes at once. That dual-classification skill is what the exam rewards. Tomorrow, EK 2.1.G: we stop looking at controls one at a time and ask why defenders layer them. A single control can be bypassed; defense in depth is what catches the adversary who gets past the first line. Review your Guided Notes — be able to classify the standard examples both ways and match each missing C-I-A principle to its harm.
